title: Chapter 33: Rooftop Highway
content: The rooftops of downtown were nothing like the ground.

Down there, the streets were canyons — narrow, dark, choked with abandoned vehicles and the shambling remnants of people who'd stopped being people. Every intersection was a choke point. Every shadow could hide a raider patrol or a feral pack or worse. The ground was Crane's kingdom, and Junko Tanaka had no intention of walking through it.

"Up," she said, and scaled the fire escape of a gutted apartment building like it was a ladder rung at a playground — fluid, tireless, her Stage 2 muscles propelling her three rungs at a time. Marcus followed, lungs burning, the Shock-Absorbing Combat Armor he'd crafted from her blueprint doing exactly nothing to make climbing easier.

Elena came behind him, field kit clanking against her back, breathing hard but refusing to complain. She'd said exactly three words since they'd left the SUV behind a block ago — "I'm fine" — and the set of her jaw suggested that challenging that assessment would be medically inadvisable.

Sera came last. She didn't climb. She *flowed* — hands and feet finding purchase with the precision of a creature that had been designed for exactly this kind of movement, her Shadow Weave dormant but her Stage 4 reflexes more than sufficient. She could have gone first. Could have scouted. But she stayed at the rear of the line, and Marcus felt why through the Bond: she was watching Junko's back.
Not out of trust. Out of assessment.

The rooftop was flat and gravel-crunchy, dotted with ventilation units and satellite dishes that hadn't broadcast anything useful in three weeks. The morning sun cut low across the cityscape, painting everything in harsh amber and deep shadow. From up here, the scale of the devastation became impossible to ignore — block after block of silent buildings, some with windows blown out, others dark and intact but empty, all of them tombs.
Junko moved to the eastern edge and pointed.
"That's Crane's."
Marcus followed her gesture and felt his stomach drop.

Four blocks to the southeast, a cluster of buildings had been transformed into something that looked disturbingly like a fortified compound. Concertina wire strung between rooftops. Searchlight mounts on the corners of what had been a department store, the bulbs dark now but the scaffolding unmistakable. Barricades at street level — vehicles stacked two deep, creating walls that funneled foot traffic toward checkpoints manned by figures with rifles.

And in the center of it all, a building that had been painted — crudely, with what looked like red house paint — with a symbol: a crane in flight, wings spread, talons extended. Victor Crane's standard, flown as openly as any warlord's banner since the fall of Rome.

**[AREA DETECTED: Victor Crane's Territory — "The Perch"]**
**[Threat Level: EXTREME]**
**[Active Patrols: 12+ armed raiders, 2-hour rotation]**
**[Recommended Action: AVOID]**

"He's got maybe two hundred fighters," Junko said, her voice flat. "Some military background, most just desperate. They rotate through those checkpoints every two hours. At night, they expand patrols outward three blocks." She pointed north. "Vale Industries Tower is eight blocks that way."

Eight blocks. Marcus could almost see it from here — the sleek dark needle of glass and steel rising above the midrise buildings, its upper floors catching the sun. Lily was up there. Breathing borrowed air. Running out of time.
"Eight blocks," he repeated. "How bad?"

"Two more of his checkpoints between here and there. Plus the park — he uses it as a kill zone. Anybody crosses that open ground, his snipers pick them off from the parking garage." Junko's grey eyes stayed fixed on the compound. "You can't go through the streets. Not with three people and a zombie queen who glows in the dark."
"I don't glow," Sera said mildly from behind them.

"Your eyes do." Junko didn't turn around. "Silver. Visible from half a block away, even in shadow. Every raider downtown knows to look for silver eyes. Crane put a bounty on an evolved zombie three days ago — alive, fifty thousand in trade goods. Dead, twenty." A pause. "You're worth more than the rest of us combined."

The information landed like a slap. Marcus felt Sera's reaction through the Bond — a controlled flare of something between alarm and calculation. She was already reassessing routes, already factoring in her own visibility as a liability.
"I can suppress the sheen," Sera said. "For short periods. It's uncomfortable, but—"

"Don't." Junko finally turned. Her grey eyes met Sera's silver ones, and Marcus felt the air between them sharpen. "If you suppress and something goes wrong, you'll be fighting at half capacity. Better to stay off the ground entirely."
"That's what the rooftops are for."

"That's what the rooftops are for." Junko held her gaze a beat longer than necessary. "I know every building between here and the Crystal Tower. After that, the gaps get wider. We'll need to come down for at least two crossings."
"Crystal Tower?"

Junko pointed northeast. A slender spire of glass rose from the skyline — beautiful and strange, its faceted surface catching and refracting the morning light in prismatic shards that scattered across the neighboring rooftops. In a city of blunt concrete and grim glass, the Crystal Tower looked like something from another world. Another era. A time when architecture was allowed to be beautiful.

"Duchamps' place," Junko said. "She's been holding the penthouse since week one. Nobody's gotten past the lobby." Something flickered across her expression — not admiration, exactly. Recognition. "She's still human. Still fighting."
Marcus filed that away. "We go over the Crystal Tower?"

"We go *past* it. The rooftop route forks there — north toward the park, east toward the financial district. North is shorter but exposed. East is longer but covered." Junko crossed her arms. "Your call."
"My call?"
"You're the one with the System." She looked at him directly for the first time since they'd started climbing. "I know the terrain. You make the decisions. That's how this works."
They moved.

Rooftop to rooftop, across narrow gaps that Junko navigated with the casual confidence of someone who'd traced this path a hundred times. Some buildings had fire escapes that served as bridges. Others were close enough to jump — close for Junko and Sera, at least. Marcus made each crossing with his heart in his throat and his hands white-knuckled on the lip of each new roof, Elena right beside him with her jaw set and her eyes forward.

From above, the city told a different story than it did from below. The zombies were obvious — slow clusters of movement in the dark streets, like oil slicks drifting through water. The raider patrols were visible too: pairs of figures moving with purpose along predetermined routes, their flashlight beams cutting through the morning haze. From up here, Marcus could see the patterns. Could plan around them.
He could also see the evidence of Crane's cruelty.

On the corner of Third and Marshall, a body hung from a lamppost — not a zombie, not anymore. A person. The sign around the neck was too far away to read, but Junko's expression when she caught him looking told him everything.

"Thief," she said. "Or at least, that's what Crane calls anyone who takes more than their ration. Last week it was a woman who stole medicine for her kid." Her voice didn't waver. "He hung her outside her own apartment so her family could watch."
"You saw this?"
"I was on this roof when they strung her up." Junko's jaw tightened. "I didn't stop them."
The confession hung in the air. Marcus didn't push. He'd learned, in three weeks of the apocalypse, that some truths needed their own time.

They stopped on a wide commercial rooftop — what had been a restaurant, its patio furniture still arranged around planters filled with dead flowers. Junko crouched at the edge, scanning the next gap, while Elena checked Marcus's pulse with the brisk efficiency of someone who wanted an excuse to stop moving for thirty seconds.
Sera appeared beside Junko.
"You haven't told him," Sera said quietly.
Junko didn't look at her. "Told him what?"
"About the yakuza."
The word dropped into the silence like a stone into still water. Marcus, two meters away, froze mid-breath. Elena's hand stilled on his wrist.
Junko's shoulders went rigid. Then, slowly, she turned her head to look at Sera with eyes that held no warmth at all.
"How do you know about that?"

"The Bond gives me heightened perception. When you were eating — when you shared that last bite — your emotional register spiked. Guilt. Shame. Old scars." Sera's silver eyes were steady. "Not combat scars. Not zombie scars. *Consequence* scars. The kind you get when you hurt people for a living and someone makes you remember."

The silence stretched. Across the rooftop, Marcus held his breath. He could feel the tension through the Bond — not just from Sera, but radiating off Junko in waves, a pressure that made the air feel thicker.

"My father was oyabun," Junko said finally. The word came out clipped, accented, dragged from somewhere deep. "Tanaka-gumi. Kobe. I was his fourth daughter and his best enforcer." Her hands curled into fists on her knees. "I broke people for a living before the dead started walking. I was good at it."
Marcus took a step forward, and Sera shot him a look — not a warning, not exactly. More like: *Let me handle this.*
"You stopped," Sera said.

"I *turned*." Junko's voice was bitter. "The infection took the choice away. When I came back — when I woke up Stage 2 — I could still remember every face. Every bone I cracked. Every time my father said 'well done, Junko' like I was a sword he'd sharpened instead of a daughter he'd raised." She exhaled roughly. "So no. I didn't stop. The virus stopped me. And now I'm a zombie who can't stop *remembering*, which is its own kind of hell."
"Is that why you stayed alone? No group, no alliance—"

"I stayed alone because I *kill* people, Seraphina." Junko's grey eyes burned. "Not zombies. People. It's what I'm *for*. It's what my father *made* me for. And the only thing standing between me and that instinct is the food your boyfriend conjures, which is the only reason I'm standing on this rooftop instead of—"
She stopped. Her jaw clenched so hard Marcus could hear her teeth grind.

"Instead of what?" Sera asked, and her voice had shifted — softer now, careful, the tone she used with Lily in her rare mentions of her sister, the tone that meant *I'm not going to hurt you, but I need you to finish that sentence.*
Junko looked at her hands. At the scars — the jagged ones, the earned ones, the ones that shimmered faintly with the silvery undertone of Stage 2 regeneration slowly erasing old damage.

"Instead of following you back to your fortress and putting my hands around the throat of everyone you care about," she said quietly. "Because that's what I do. I find the people my target loves, and I—"

"That's what you *did*." Sera's voice cut through like a blade. "You were a weapon. Weapons don't choose their targets. Your father did. The yakuza did. And now you're standing on a rooftop in the apocalypse telling me you're dangerous, when the only thing I've seen you do is *share food with a stranger*."
The words hit Junko like a physical blow. Marcus saw it — the flinch, the moment of raw vulnerability before the walls slammed back up.
"You don't know me," Junko said.

"I know you could have killed Marcus and taken every scrap of Spirit Meat for yourself. I know you didn't. I know you sat on a curb in the rain and ate like a person who'd forgotten what it felt like to be treated like one." Sera stepped closer, and her silver eyes held Junko's grey ones with an intensity that Marcus recognized from their own early days — from the apartment, from the first feeding, from the moment a starving zombie had chosen trust over instinct. "I know you're scared of what you are. And I know that fear is the only honest thing about you."

Junko's throat worked. For a moment, Marcus thought she might strike — the coiled tension in her frame, the predator's instinct screaming at the proximity. But she didn't move. She just stared at Sera with those grey eyes, and something in them cracked.
"You love him," Junko said. Not an accusation. An observation.
"Yes."
"And you're not... afraid? That I'll—"

"I'm terrified." Sera's voice was quiet and absolute. "Every time he gets close to another woman, every time the System flashes a new target, every time I feel his attention shift even one degree — I feel it. The Bond carries his emotions, and when he feels curiosity about someone else, I feel it like a knife under my ribs." She paused. "But fear isn't the same as ownership. And love isn't the same as control. He chose me. He keeps choosing me. And if I try to lock him in a cage to keep him safe, I become the thing I was afraid of."

Junko stared at her for a long moment. Then, slowly, something in her expression shifted — the rigid defense of a woman who'd spent her entire life being a weapon, encountering the unfamiliar shape of being *understood*.
"You're strange," she said.
"Dead woman walking," Sera replied. "Strangeness is the least of my problems."
Almost — *almost* — Junko smiled.
Eight blocks away, Vale Industries Tower pierced the morning sky. Close enough to see. Too far to reach without crossing Crane's territory on the ground.

"What do we do?" Elena asked, staring at the tower through binoculars scavenged from the restaurant's manager office. "We can't go around — that adds hours. We can't go through — that adds casualties. And we're on a clock."

Marcus looked at the tower. Through the Bond, Sera's urgency pressed against his awareness — Lily's fading signal, ninety-six hours becoming ninety-four, every minute a breath her sister might not have.
"The Crystal Tower," he said. "If the rooftops fork there, maybe there's a path I'm not seeing. We go past it, reassess, find a way through."
Junko nodded once. "It's the best route. But Marcu — there's something you should know about the Crystal Tower."
"Tell me on the way."

She stood, brushed gravel from her pants, and moved to the eastern edge of the roof. The morning light caught her silhouette — lean, precise, the silhouette of a weapon still learning it didn't have to cut.
"The woman who lives there," Junko said without turning around. "She's the proudest person I've ever met. Prouder than my father. Prouder than anyCEO." A beat. "She'll try to kill you too."
Marcus shouldered his pack and followed the blade into the light.
"Getting used to that," he said.
